item.title: Watch Mehdi's Powerful Speech in Defense of Independent Journalism at the Zeteo Second Birthday Party
item.description: Dear friends, We are now officially two years old. Happy birthday to us! At our second birthday party at the Au Za’atar restaurant in New York, surrounded by an audience of politicians, activists, celebrities and, of course, lots of journalists, I laid out my vision for independent journalism and…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Dear friends,

We are now officially two years old. Happy birthday to us!

At our second birthday party at the Au Za’atar restaurant in New York, surrounded by an audience of politicians, activists, celebrities and, of course, lots of journalists, I laid out my vision for independent journalism and the key goals of Zeteo.

Despite having given hundreds, perhaps thousands, of speeches over my 47 years on this planet, I’ll be very honest with you: Zohran Mamdani is a hard act to follow. I was a little nervous speaking right after him.

Nevertheless, do give my remarks above a watch. I explained how Zeteo’s mission is to tell the truth “no matter how awkward, or uncomfortable, or controversial,” and not “both-sidesing” or “euphemizing” the truth, whether it is about fascism at home or genocide abroad.

Independent journalism requires support. Please consider becoming a paid subscriber - even a founding member - as we celebrate this second anniversary of Zeteo. And if you’re already a paid subscriber, consider donating to Zeteo to further support our work and our growth.

I have to say: Zeteo could not have reached this milestone, two years!, had it not been for the support and encouragement of all of you: our subscribers, our friends, our family. Thank you!

Onwards and upwards!

Best wishes,

Mehdi

P.S. Keep an eye on your inbox in the coming days for exclusive pictures from our second birthday party. You can see which big-name politicians, celebs, and journalists attended and celebrated with us!

Other Zeteo stories you may have missed:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: Fascists Want Comedian Fired
item.description: On this day in 1967, Muhammad Ali refused to be inducted into the US Army and was stripped of his heavyweight boxing title. “The real enemies of my people are right here, not in Vietnam,” he said. Good morning! Andrew here, to talk about Donald Trump ’s latest effort to get Jimmy Kimmel fired. The…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1967, Muhammad Ali refused to be inducted into the US Army and was stripped of his heavyweight boxing title. “The real enemies of my people are right here, not in Vietnam,” he said.

Good morning! Andrew here, to talk about Donald Trump’s latest effort to get Jimmy Kimmel fired. The comedian’s response is well worth reading. More on that below.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ as Stephen Miller once said, only fascists are enraged by the idea of free speech. Plus, Republicans are using super PAC trickery to try to pick the Democrats who run against them, and Ron DeSantis wants to delete four Democratic congressional seats.

In September, President Donald Trump and MAGA managed to get Jimmy Kimmel taken off the air by twisting his words. Now, the president – who once pledged he would “bring back free speech in America” – is again demanding Kimmel be fired, this time for telling a mean joke.

Thanks to public outcry, Kimmel’s previous suspension was only temporary – and ever since Disney and ABC brought him back, he’s been unleashing on Trump with abandon. Last Thursday’s episode was no different, when Kimmel donned a tuxedo and performed a mock version of a roast that a comedian might offer at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, “if our president wasn’t a trembling drama queen who’s scared of comedy.”

Kimmel, as is the case these days, did not hold back. After joking about the president’s age and visibly declining health, and the possibility he “has a medical emergency tonight,” Kimmel turned to the first lady. “Look at Melania Trump, so beautiful,” he said, as video of the first lady sitting in a crowd played on the screen. “Mrs. Trump, you have a glow like an expectant widow.”

The comments attracted little controversy at first, until… well… you’ve probably heard by now: An apparent gunman tried to storm the White House Correspondents’ Dinner on Saturday, with multiple shots fired after he raced through the security perimeter inside the Washington Hilton – leading the Secret Service to usher Trump, Melania, and top officials out of the gala, attendees to duck down amid the chaos, and the festivities to end early.

The unfortunate turn of events gave Trump and his team an opportunity to again call for Kimmel’s firing.

Melania Trump stepped up first, saying: “A coward, Kimmel hides behind ABC because he knows the network will keep running cover to protect him. Enough is enough. It is time for ABC to take a stand.”

In a statement she almost certainly didn’t write herself, the first lady claimed that “Kimmel’s hateful and violent rhetoric is intended to divide our country,” and that “his words are corrosive and deepens the political sickness within America” – as if her husband isn’t the country’s #1, unquestioned, biggest hatemonger. Trump regularly calls his political opponents “terrorists,” “garbage,” and the “enemy from within.” He’s accused Democratic lawmakers of “SEDITIOUS BEHAVIOR, punishable by DEATH.” (Just watch this video we put together!)

Trump – the man who just recently threatened to end a “whole civilization” in Iran, a nation of over 90 million people – wrote a post accusing the late-night comedian of issuing a “despicable call to violence,” adding: “Jimmy Kimmel should be immediately fired by Disney and ABC.”

Never mind the president’s ode to “free speech in our Constitution” during his press briefing Saturday evening, after evacuating the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.

No one on Earth believed he meant any of it, not after all of the effort he’s expended trying to jail journalists, sue and shake down media organizations, censor late-night hosts, and deport foreign students who oppose Israel’s genocide in Gaza.

That hasn’t stopped right-wing journalists or some liberal pundits from playing along.

“Trump can be a rude provocateur. Kimmel is a sadist masquerading as a comedian,” tweeted longtime Fox reporter Geraldo Rivera.

David Axelrod, the former chief strategist for Barack Obama, posted that Kimmel is “courageous at a time when there has been too much cowardice” – but suggested he apologize anyway for his “tasteless joke.” Fox eagerly wrote up his advice.

Kimmel didn’t have kind words to say in his mock roast about Stephen Miller, Trump’s chief policy architect: “Stephen Miller is so racist – the reason he went bald is because his hair was black,” he joked.

We don’t have many nice words to say, either. We’ll say this, though, about Miller, who tweeted in 2022, “If the idea of free speech enrages you – the cornerstone of democratic self-government – than I regret to inform you that you are a fascist.”

Then, and only then, he had a point.

But, of course, we’re all used to Republican hypocrisy and shamelessness at this point, right?

Kimmel addressed the calls for his firing on his show late Monday. He did not apologize. Instead, he offered a smart point of his own.

“I understand that the first lady had a stressful experience over the weekend,” Kimmel said. “Most of all, I agree that painful and violent rhetoric is something we should reject… I think a great place to start to dial that back would be to have a conversation with your husband about it.”

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian state media on Sunday reported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,521 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 35 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

Negotiation rejections: As Israel continues to bomb southern Lebanon, despite a ceasefire, Hezbollah maintains its disapproval of Lebanon’s peace negotiations with Israel and says it will continue to confront Israeli aggression.

Economic fury: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned that companies that provide services to Iran’s airlines may be subject to US sanctions.

Ally in Putin: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met with Russian President Vladimir Putin, who reportedly told him that he would do “everything” to help achieve peace in the country.

With two months left in Maine’s Senate primary, a super PAC backing Republican Senator Susan Collins is spending $2 million on attack ads against Graham Platner, the veteran and oysterman who’s leading Janet Mills in the polls.

The group, Pine Tree Results PAC, has received at least $4.3 million in dark money, and millions more from billionaire donors. Steve Schwarzman, whose private equity firm Blackstone Group is one of the world’s largest landlords, gave the group $2 million. Schwarzman made the donation a day before Collins voted to advance Trump’s tax bill, which was chock-full of tax breaks for the ultra-wealthy.

Pine Tree Results accepted $1 million from a dark money group affiliated with Leonard Leo, the right-wing operative who built the Supreme Court’s far-right supermajority, which eliminated the federal right to an abortion. Pine Tree Results also received $500,000 apiece from Jude and Christopher Reyes, brothers who co-chair the beverage distributor Reyes Holdings, which benefited from a special exemption from Trump’s ruinous tariffs.

Pine Tree Results is chaired by Scott Reed, corporate records show. Reed, a former top strategist for the US Chamber of Commerce, chaired a different pro-Collins super PAC in 2020.

In Ohio, a super PAC funded by GOP Rep. Max Miller’s father is trying to pick his Democratic opponent, ahead of next week’s primary.

The group, Jobs & Prosperity PAC, has received $50,000 from Miller’s dad and another $50,000 from his dad’s investment firm. The super PAC recently sent out texts calling Dem candidate Ed Fitzgerald “too liberal,” in an apparent effort to boost his campaign against Brian Poindexter, who’s backed by Bernie Sanders.

Billionaire tax on the ballot: California will vote this year on a one-time 5% tax on billionaires to fund public services, after the proposal garnered 1.5 million signatures to qualify for the ballot. Tech moguls have already spent tens of millions to oppose the tax and have threatened to leave California. Dem Governor Gavin Newsom, a possible 2028 contender, also opposes the tax.

Longest partial shutdown: Speaker Mike Johnson is teeing up a vote Wednesday on a revised Homeland Security funding bill, seeking to remove language that excludes funding for ICE and Border Patrol. If passed, it would further delay an end to the 10-week-long DHS shutdown, as the new measure would need to be passed by the Senate.

Ron’s redistricting: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis leaked his proposed gerrymandered congressional map, which is designed to remove four Democratic seats, before the state legislature convenes today. The state’s constitution explicitly prohibits political gerrymandering.

Ballroom finds new funding: Republicans are going to bat for Trump’s lavish ballroom in the wake of the attack on the White House Correspondents’ Dinner. GOP Senator Lindsey Graham told reporters the party plans to introduce a bill to grant $400 million for the construction of the ballroom, which Trump has promised would not be funded by taxpayers.

Floridians passed the “Fair Districts” amendment in 2010 to stop exactly what DeSantis is doing right now with his mid-decade redistricting. What percent of voters approved the amendment?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Zeteo’s John Harwood talks with election-law ace Marc Elias TODAY at 4pm ET (1pm PT/9pm GMT) about how Trump and his party want to manipulate the 2026 outcome and whether defenders of democracy can stop them.

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel’s genocide has left at least 96% of Gaza’s cropland damaged or inaccessible, the aid group Mercy Corps found. Separately, Israel killed a 9-year-old boy in Khan Younis today.

🇸🇩 RSF holding doctors : Sudan Doctors Network

🇦🇫🇵🇰 Ceasefire in shambles: Violence between Afghanistan and Pakistan re-ignited, putting their fragile ceasefire deal in jeopardy. Afghanistan says four were killed in eastern Kunar, and Pakistani officials allege that three civilians were injured in South Waziristan.

🇮🇶 New prime minister: Political newcomer Ali al-Zaidi was nominated by a coalition of Shia parties to be Iraq’s next prime minister after former Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki and incumbent Prime Minister Mohammed Shia al-Sudani withdrew their bids for the seat.

🇮🇩 Deadly train crash: Four people were killed, and dozens were injured, after a long-distance train hit a commuter train in Indonesia.


Watch a far-right Republican congressman get humbled by a class of students as he spews propaganda to justify spending billions on unnecessary wars.

Zeteo’s Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1967, Muhammad Ali refused to be inducted into the US Army and was stripped of his heavyweight boxing title. “The real enemies of my people are right here, not in Vietnam,” he said.

Good morning! Andrew here, to talk about Donald Trump’s latest effort to get Jimmy Kimmel fired. The comedian’s response is well worth reading. More on that below.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ as Stephen Miller once said, only fascists are enraged by the idea of free speech. Plus, Republicans are using super PAC trickery to try to pick the Democrats who run against them, and Ron DeSantis wants to delete four Democratic congressional seats.

In September, President Donald Trump and MAGA managed to get Jimmy Kimmel taken off the air by twisting his words. Now, the president – who once pledged he would “bring back free speech in America” – is again demanding Kimmel be fired, this time for telling a mean joke.

Thanks to public outcry, Kimmel’s previous suspension was only temporary – and ever since Disney and ABC brought him back, he’s been unleashing on Trump with abandon. Last Thursday’s episode was no different, when Kimmel donned a tuxedo and performed a mock version of a roast that a comedian might offer at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, “if our president wasn’t a trembling drama queen who’s scared of comedy.”

Kimmel, as is the case these days, did not hold back. After joking about the president’s age and visibly declining health, and the possibility he “has a medical emergency tonight,” Kimmel turned to the first lady. “Look at Melania Trump, so beautiful,” he said, as video of the first lady sitting in a crowd played on the screen. “Mrs. Trump, you have a glow like an expectant widow.”

The comments attracted little controversy at first, until… well… you’ve probably heard by now: An apparent gunman tried to storm the White House Correspondents’ Dinner on Saturday, with multiple shots fired after he raced through the security perimeter inside the Washington Hilton – leading the Secret Service to usher Trump, Melania, and top officials out of the gala, attendees to duck down amid the chaos, and the festivities to end early.

The unfortunate turn of events gave Trump and his team an opportunity to again call for Kimmel’s firing.

Melania Trump stepped up first, saying: “A coward, Kimmel hides behind ABC because he knows the network will keep running cover to protect him. Enough is enough. It is time for ABC to take a stand.”

In a statement she almost certainly didn’t write herself, the first lady claimed that “Kimmel’s hateful and violent rhetoric is intended to divide our country,” and that “his words are corrosive and deepens the political sickness within America” – as if her husband isn’t the country’s #1, unquestioned, biggest hatemonger. Trump regularly calls his political opponents “terrorists,” “garbage,” and the “enemy from within.” He’s accused Democratic lawmakers of “SEDITIOUS BEHAVIOR, punishable by DEATH.” (Just watch this video we put together!)

Trump – the man who just recently threatened to end a “whole civilization” in Iran, a nation of over 90 million people – wrote a post accusing the late-night comedian of issuing a “despicable call to violence,” adding: “Jimmy Kimmel should be immediately fired by Disney and ABC.”

Never mind the president’s ode to “free speech in our Constitution” during his press briefing Saturday evening, after evacuating the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.

No one on Earth believed he meant any of it, not after all of the effort he’s expended trying to jail journalists, sue and shake down media organizations, censor late-night hosts, and deport foreign students who oppose Israel’s genocide in Gaza.

That hasn’t stopped right-wing journalists or some liberal pundits from playing along.

“Trump can be a rude provocateur. Kimmel is a sadist masquerading as a comedian,” tweeted longtime Fox reporter Geraldo Rivera.

David Axelrod, the former chief strategist for Barack Obama, posted that Kimmel is “courageous at a time when there has been too much cowardice” – but suggested he apologize anyway for his “tasteless joke.” Fox eagerly wrote up his advice.

Kimmel didn’t have kind words to say in his mock roast about Stephen Miller, Trump’s chief policy architect: “Stephen Miller is so racist – the reason he went bald is because his hair was black,” he joked.

We don’t have many nice words to say, either. We’ll say this, though, about Miller, who tweeted in 2022, “If the idea of free speech enrages you – the cornerstone of democratic self-government – than I regret to inform you that you are a fascist.”

Then, and only then, he had a point.

But, of course, we’re all used to Republican hypocrisy and shamelessness at this point, right?

Kimmel addressed the calls for his firing on his show late Monday. He did not apologize. Instead, he offered a smart point of his own.

“I understand that the first lady had a stressful experience over the weekend,” Kimmel said. “Most of all, I agree that painful and violent rhetoric is something we should reject… I think a great place to start to dial that back would be to have a conversation with your husband about it.”

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian state media on Sunday reported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,521 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 35 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

Negotiation rejections: As Israel continues to bomb southern Lebanon, despite a ceasefire, Hezbollah maintains its disapproval of Lebanon’s peace negotiations with Israel and says it will continue to confront Israeli aggression.

Economic fury: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned that companies that provide services to Iran’s airlines may be subject to US sanctions.

Ally in Putin: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met with Russian President Vladimir Putin, who reportedly told him that he would do “everything” to help achieve peace in the country.

With two months left in Maine’s Senate primary, a super PAC backing Republican Senator Susan Collins is spending $2 million on attack ads against Graham Platner, the veteran and oysterman who’s leading Janet Mills in the polls.

The group, Pine Tree Results PAC, has received at least $4.3 million in dark money, and millions more from billionaire donors. Steve Schwarzman, whose private equity firm Blackstone Group is one of the world’s largest landlords, gave the group $2 million. Schwarzman made the donation a day before Collins voted to advance Trump’s tax bill, which was chock-full of tax breaks for the ultra-wealthy.

Pine Tree Results accepted $1 million from a dark money group affiliated with Leonard Leo, the right-wing operative who built the Supreme Court’s far-right supermajority, which eliminated the federal right to an abortion. Pine Tree Results also received $500,000 apiece from Jude and Christopher Reyes, brothers who co-chair the beverage distributor Reyes Holdings, which benefited from a special exemption from Trump’s ruinous tariffs.

Pine Tree Results is chaired by Scott Reed, corporate records show. Reed, a former top strategist for the US Chamber of Commerce, chaired a different pro-Collins super PAC in 2020.

In Ohio, a super PAC funded by GOP Rep. Max Miller’s father is trying to pick his Democratic opponent, ahead of next week’s primary.

The group, Jobs & Prosperity PAC, has received $50,000 from Miller’s dad and another $50,000 from his dad’s investment firm. The super PAC recently sent out texts calling Dem candidate Ed Fitzgerald “too liberal,” in an apparent effort to boost his campaign against Brian Poindexter, who’s backed by Bernie Sanders.

Billionaire tax on the ballot: California will vote this year on a one-time 5% tax on billionaires to fund public services, after the proposal garnered 1.5 million signatures to qualify for the ballot. Tech moguls have already spent tens of millions to oppose the tax and have threatened to leave California. Dem Governor Gavin Newsom, a possible 2028 contender, also opposes the tax.

Longest partial shutdown: Speaker Mike Johnson is teeing up a vote Wednesday on a revised Homeland Security funding bill, seeking to remove language that excludes funding for ICE and Border Patrol. If passed, it would further delay an end to the 10-week-long DHS shutdown, as the new measure would need to be passed by the Senate.

Ron’s redistricting: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis leaked his proposed gerrymandered congressional map, which is designed to remove four Democratic seats, before the state legislature convenes today. The state’s constitution explicitly prohibits political gerrymandering.

Ballroom finds new funding: Republicans are going to bat for Trump’s lavish ballroom in the wake of the attack on the White House Correspondents’ Dinner. GOP Senator Lindsey Graham told reporters the party plans to introduce a bill to grant $400 million for the construction of the ballroom, which Trump has promised would not be funded by taxpayers.

Floridians passed the “Fair Districts” amendment in 2010 to stop exactly what DeSantis is doing right now with his mid-decade redistricting. What percent of voters approved the amendment?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Zeteo’s John Harwood talks with election-law ace Marc Elias TODAY at 4pm ET (1pm PT/9pm GMT) about how Trump and his party want to manipulate the 2026 outcome and whether defenders of democracy can stop them.

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel’s genocide has left at least 96% of Gaza’s cropland damaged or inaccessible, the aid group Mercy Corps found. Separately, Israel killed a 9-year-old boy in Khan Younis today.

🇸🇩 RSF holding doctors : Sudan Doctors Network

🇦🇫🇵🇰 Ceasefire in shambles: Violence between Afghanistan and Pakistan re-ignited, putting their fragile ceasefire deal in jeopardy. Afghanistan says four were killed in eastern Kunar, and Pakistani officials allege that three civilians were injured in South Waziristan.

🇮🇶 New prime minister: Political newcomer Ali al-Zaidi was nominated by a coalition of Shia parties to be Iraq’s next prime minister after former Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki and incumbent Prime Minister Mohammed Shia al-Sudani withdrew their bids for the seat.

🇮🇩 Deadly train crash: Four people were killed, and dozens were injured, after a long-distance train hit a commuter train in Indonesia.


Watch a far-right Republican congressman get humbled by a class of students as he spews propaganda to justify spending billions on unnecessary wars.

Zeteo’s Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: 7 Times Trump Influenced Markets and Investors Bet Big
item.description: Amid all of US President Donald Trump’s chronic self-contradictions, his self-serving desire to shape the global economy is a rare constant. The oil crisis stoked by Trump’s war on Iran may have exposed the limits of such efforts, but his public statements and social media posts on the war show he…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Amid all of US President Donald Trump’s chronic self-contradictions, his self-serving desire to shape the global economy is a rare constant. The oil crisis stoked by Trump’s war on Iran may have exposed the limits of such efforts, but his public statements and social media posts on the war show he maintains his aim to bend markets to his will.

Furthermore, as Trump sends prices dropping or soaring, anonymous traders seem to be turning foreknowledge of executive actions into financial gain.

While the nation’s wealthiest exploit stock and prediction markets, regulatory bodies are increasingly uninterested in stopping financial crimes. The number of enforcement cases filed by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is down 20% from those pursued by his predecessor under the Biden administration. The Trump-appointed SEC chair, Paul Atkins, recently refused to say that insider trading is always bad. Over at the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, the enforcement agency for futures markets, the current head created an “innovation task force” for industries key to the Trump family. Ben Schiffrin, a former attorney at the SEC, notes that Atkins is prioritizing the promotion of AI, crypto, and prediction markets ahead of his regulatory responsibilities.

In these free-for-all conditions, Zeteo is breaking down seven instances of suspicious trading resulting from the president’s statements:

In March 2025, ahead of Trump’s various tariff announcements, including the sweeping “Liberation Day” statement that tanked the market, multiple executive branch officials made “well-timed trades.” As ProPublica reported, such traders included a top US trade official and multiple longtime State Department officials. A US ambassador, Gautam Rana, sold between $830,000 and $1.7 million worth of stock in two weeks ahead of Trump’s announcement.

Tyler Gellasch, who runs the Healthy Markets Association, an ethics group, told ProPublica that such trading “undermines trust in government and the markets” and makes “our markets look rigged.”

“BE COOL!” Trump posted to Truth Social in April 2025, as markets roiled from his imposition of steep global tariffs. He later added, “THIS IS A GREAT TIME TO BUY!!! DJT”

Less than four hours later, Trump initialized a 90-day pause on nearly all tariffs. The market rebounded, regaining approximately 70% of losses from the four previous trading days.

“DJT” is both the president’s occasional sign-off and the stock symbol for his media company. The ambiguity likely contributed to a 23% increase in Trump Media and Technology Group’s stock price that day.

Former White House ethics lawyer Richard Painter told PBS that Trump is “loving this, this control over markets, but he better be careful,” adding that trade using insider information or informing others is unlawful.

Kathleen Clark, an ethics law expert at Washington University School of Law, told the same outlet that Trump’s statement would be grounds for an investigation under any other administration.

“He’s sending the message that he can effectively and with impunity manipulate the market,” Clark said.

In October 2025, Trump flexed his typing thumbs again, posting, “One of the Policies that we are calculating at this moment is a massive increase of Tariffs on Chinese products coming into the United States of America.” Stocks in US rare earth companies rose collectively.

In the months prior, the government bought stock in large domestic rare earth startups and the Department of Defense bypassed contracting laws to forge a secretive, multi-billion-dollar deal with MP Materials, becoming its largest shareholder.

In January, a newly-created, anonymous account on Polymarket made almost half a million dollars on various bids that the then Venezuelan president, Nicolás Maduro, would be toppled by Jan. 31. The majority of the trader’s bids were made hours before the Venezuelan president was seized, on Jan. 3.

The position’s odds, 6.5% on Jan. 2, rose to around 12.5% just before Maduro’s capture the next morning. These trades had “all the hallmarks” of insider knowledge, the CEO of Better Markets, a financial reform non-profit, told CBS.

Indeed, a US special forces soldier involved in Maduro’s capture was arrested last week in alleged connection to the trade. In response, Trump stated, “That’s like Pete Rose betting on his own team,” in reference to the disgraced gambler and baseball player. “Now, if he bet against his team, that would be no good, but he bet on his own team. I’ll look into it,” he claimed.

Trump expressed both disapproval of and capitulation to prediction markets: “The whole world, unfortunately, has become somewhat of a casino,” the former Atlantic City casino owner said. “I was never much in favor of it. I don’t like it conceptually, but it is what it is now.”

In late February, six accounts won around $1.2 million through Polymarket bets on Trump’s initial Iran attack. The accounts had no prior activity, were funded through similar paths, most within the day, and bet specifically that the US would strike on Feb. 28, making “yes” buys just hours before the attack. It was seen as suspicious that one of the same accounts, with eight others, also made bets on there being a ceasefire by March 31. However, Trump didn’t announce a ceasefire until April 7.

Insider trading regulations, already weak in relation to stock markets, are even more limited regarding prediction platforms. Donald Trump Jr. holds advisory roles for Kalshi and Polymarket, with payment from the former and investment in the latter. The Trump administration has defended the platform against state lawsuits.

It is challenging to identify dodgy accounts due to the mechanics of blockchain technology, which allow individuals to act with anonymity and create multiple wallets under new users. Suspicious activity is only trackable in hindsight by sifting through vast quantities of on-chain data.

President Trump doesn’t know the “art of the deal” – he didn’t even really write his own book of that title. But he does know how to post falsehoods to Truth Social, as he did in late March, describing “VERY GOOD AND PRODUCTIVE CONVERSATIONS” with Iran, three weeks into the war, also claiming discussions towards “A COMPLETE AND TOTAL RESOLUTION OF HOSTILITIES” were ongoing.

The market responded. The S&P 500 leapt a remarkable 240 points. But Trump was reportedly losing sway over investors, and when Iranian leadership denied engaging in any talks, upward curves bent back towards reality, with a $3 trillion market cap swing in under an hour.

More than half a billion dollars reportedly changed hands in the 15 minutes prior to Trump’s announcement, and trading volume was nine times the average at that same time during the previous five days, according to Bloomberg data. While the administration insisted that Trump has a no-tolerance policy for “official illegal profiteering,” the patterns are telling.

Traders bought $1.5 billion in S&P 500 futures and sold $192 million in oil futures, according to a stock market watcher. Analysts and experienced traders indicate that the highly unusual nature of these trades suggested insider knowledge.

“Who was it? Trump? A family member? A White House staffer? This is corruption. Mind blowing corruption,” Senator Chris Murphy (D-Conn.) wrote on Twitter.

Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the war, choking off the flow of oil, spiking energy prices, and throttling the global economy. Trump’s energy secretary attempted some crisis-management of his own. Chris Wright, a former fracking executive, made a surprise statement on social media, claiming that the US Navy “successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing.”

He deleted the post within minutes, but in the interim, “oil prices slid at their steepest pace in years,” reported the Wall Street Journal. The White House denied Wright’s claims, scapegoating a staffer it said “incorrectly captioned” the post. The price of oil shot up again, resulting in a “whipsaw market.” The US Oil Fund lost approximately $84 million.

Such extreme market fluctuation is exacerbated by trends in AI-generated sentiment analysis and high-frequency trading, enabling statements by senior officials to move billions in a matter of minutes.

Investors have come to expect this insider profiteering. “So who just made $100 million shorting oil for the three minutes that Chris Wright had that post up?” speculated Spencer Hakimian, a hedge fund manager.

Researcher Matt Stoller noted that market manipulation by Trump officials “isn’t entirely without precedent, but the speed and brazenness does seem new.”

Zeteo’s Alexa Cohen contributed to this story.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: Can We Call a Fascist a Fascist? Prem and Swin on the Right’s ‘Concern’ Over Political Violence
item.description: In the wake of an alleged gunman’s attempt to breach the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, the incendiary Republican Party is now… blaming the left for “inciting violence.” How are Republicans (who have been busy vilifying Americans, aiding Israel’s genocide, and supporting masked agents…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
In the wake of an alleged gunman’s attempt to breach the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, the incendiary Republican Party is now… blaming the left for “inciting violence.”

How are Republicans (who have been busy vilifying Americans, aiding Israel’s genocide, and supporting masked agents kidnapping and murdering people blaming others for violence? What is up with their obsession with Trump’s ballroom? How is the media (once again!) both-sidesing it all, and peddling Republican talking points?

“The God Emperor Donald Trump wants the ballroom. So this violent incident from Saturday night at this glitzy affair in Northwest Washington, DC is the perfect incident to try to turn that into an ad campaign, basically, led by the upper crust of the Republican Party to try get… this corrupt ballroom that basically nobody else asked for, shoved down [the American people’s] throats,” says Swin.

Prem adds: “For those who are only now condemning political violence, and who are now using this alleged gunman to argue for giving DHS even more money, you want to talk about political violence? DHS – the reason why it’s so unpopular is because just this year, two of its agents murdered Americans in broad daylight. Two masked agents carrying out a political agenda carried out violence. If that’s not political violence, I’m not sure what is.”

Whenever political violence against Trump, or against some of his most prominent allies, occurs, national Democratic Party leaders always rush to condemn the shootings or incidents. Conversely, Trump and his allies routinely encourage political violence, and this president openly celebrates the deaths of his political and cultural enemies. The GOP also regularly calls their liberal opponents “fascists,” and refuse to tame their rhetoric even when it’s tied to racist hysteria that directly fuels murders.

But whenever a violent event occurs where they are threatened – as happened on Saturday night in Washington, DC, during an alleged assassination attempt against Trump officials at the annual White House Correspondents’ Dinner – virtually everyone in the upper tiers of Trumpland and the conservative movement instantly starts whining about how the Democrats caused this by correctly diagnosing Trump’s authoritarianism.

It’s the classic move of a spoiled bully: The moment they face even the slightest pushback, they play the victim and demand special treatment. That’s exactly what’s happening today – and it is a choice to fall for their transparently bad-faith efforts to get people to stop calling out Trump and his cohorts for what they are.

“Those who constantly, falsely label and slander the president as a fascist and threat to democracy and compare to Hitler to score political points are fueling this kind of violence,” Karoline Leavitt, Trump’s White House press secretary, said at a press briefing Monday.

The president, the White House, the Republican National Committee, and other major GOP organizations and elected officials are now rushing to make the same claim – and they’re doing it on behalf of a man with his own ever-rising body count.

As Swin notes on this week’s episode of ‘Ask The Editor,’ he’s spoken with several senior Trump administration officials and other advisers to the president who expressed their frustrations at how ever since Trump was nearly assassinated in 2024, the Republican Party has largely failed to paint the Democrats as the party of political violence. These senior Republicans look at the polling and see what everyone else does: The country is deeply divided, with essentially one half of the electorate always blaming the other side for political violence, and vice versa.

Some of these sources, though, are a little bit more self-aware, albeit in an anonymous, private context. They concede that there’s more than enough blame to go around on their side – and that, because Trump so frequently revels in encouraging, celebrating, or making light of violence against his foes, it is “sometimes hard to make the case” that the left is driving the political bloodlust in the US, in the words of one senior Trump administration appointee.

Imagine that.

‘Ask the Editor’ is Zeteo’s weekly Q&A show where subscribers can tune in live on Zeteo.com and YouTube. Watch the full video above to hear Prem and Swin unpack it all.

Let us know what you think in the comments, and subscribe to catch our next episode of Ask the Editor on Mondays at 11am ET on Zeteo.com, YouTube, or on the Substack app.

If you believe in the work we do and want to see more of it, do consider becoming a paid subscriber. A donation to Zeteo also goes a long way.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: 🇺🇸 The Land of Endless Political Violence
item.description: On this day in 2011, President Barack Obama released a copy of his long-form birth certificate to silence racist conspiracy theorists, including Donald Trump. “I am really honored, and I am really proud that I was able to do something that nobody else could do,” Trump responded. Good morning,…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2011, President Barack Obama released a copy of his long-form birth certificate to silence racist conspiracy theorists, including Donald Trump. “I am really honored, and I am really proud that I was able to do something that nobody else could do,” Trump responded.

Good morning, friends. Prem and Andrew here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday morning wherever you are. Today, we need to talk about “incitement,” political violence, and who is actually desensitized to violence: the American people, or Washington, DC?

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we review the bizarre DC political and media response to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner gunman, Israel’s violence in Lebanon shatters any illusion of a “ceasefire,” and the Senate Democratic primary races in Maine and Michigan heat up.

“I’ve been coming to DC for almost 16 years, trying to make change. And I’m hoping this is the last time I have to come.” That’s what Judi Richardson, who lost her daughter to gun violence in 2010, said in front of the US Capitol last week. Richardson was among several family members of gun victims whom Prem followed around Congress as they begged our elected leaders to finally do something about America’s unique and horrifying epidemic.

They only had a handful of meetings, and the visit drew little attention. There was no momentum to be found on Capitol Hill for gun reform last week.

Days later, across the city, a gunman attempted to breach the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, with multiple shots being fired by law enforcement as he ran through the security perimeter inside the Washington Hilton. The incident – which took place at a glitzy media gala attended by President Donald Trump, his top officials, and titans of industries – set off a firestorm of controversy.

Trump and Republicans are using the shooting as fodder to support building the president’s pet project – a massive, corporate-funded White House ballroom. Even before Trump addressed the public, the talking points already seemed to be circulating.

“I don’t want to hear one more fucking criticism of Trump’s new ballroom at the White House,” conservative pundit Meghan McCain tweeted, setting off an avalanche of similar tweets from right-wing influencers and politicians – even though, to be clear, the White House Correspondents’ Dinner is a media event, hosted by an independent organization, and is unlikely to be hosted in the White House ballroom, were it to be completed.

We’re also seeing right-wing politicians and media figures laboring to blame the apparent assassination attempt on rhetoric from Democrats. And we’re seeing concerns from Beltway journalists about how Americans – who live in a nation with more guns than people, and the only country in the world where mass shootings are so commonplace – have been “desensitized” to “political violence.”

What planet have these people been living on?

Among the examples of supposedly worrying rhetoric from Democrats: Michigan Senator Elissa Slotkin called Trump an “existential threat to democracy” (which is correct), and Minnesota Governor Tim Walz described Trump as “fascist to his core” (fact check: also true). The Republican National Committee accused both of incitement, just weeks after Trump called Democrats “terrorists” (again) and threatened the genocide of 93 million people in Iran.

Montana Senator Tim Sheehy, for his part, accused the New York Times of “amplifying Islamic terrorists who demand violence against people they disagree with.” This is the same senator who, last month, helped break the arm of Brian McGinnis – a veteran who went to Congress to protest the US-Israeli war on Iran.

CNN’s Dana Bash (who herself has viciously compared pro-Palestine students to Nazis) on Sunday asked Democratic Rep. Jamie Raskin if he’s concerned about Democrats using rhetoric such as that Trump is “terrible for this country and so on.”

Of course, there’s no evidence that Democrats or the left are responsible for incidents like this. And Trump’s past alleged assailants have a mixed background. Thomas Matthew Crooks, who grazed Trump’s ear with a bullet in Butler, Pennsylvania, was a registered Republican.

Political violence is, in reality, a bipartisan issue - though studies show it is more of a right-wing than a left-wing problem. Minnesota Democratic lawmaker Melissa Hortman was murdered last year by a Trump supporter. In 2011, Democratic Rep. Gabby Giffords was shot in the head by a murderous far-right conspiracist. A left-wing activist shot GOP House Majority Leader Steve Scalise in 2017. Right-wing podcaster Charlie Kirk was assassinated at a college event in Utah last year.

Trump, for his part, is America’s inciter-in-chief. He regularly accuses Democratic politicians of treason and sedition. He has called for Democratic lawmakers to be executed. He helped foment the deadly Jan. 6 riot at the US Capitol. And yet Sunday night, Trump said that “the hate speech of the Democrats is very dangerous.”

Posts from the anti-LGBTQ social media account Libs of TikTok have preceded scores of bomb threats nationwide; that far-right account is now baselessly and hypocritically trying to pin the DC dinner attack on progressive Rep. Ilhan Omar’s statements and left-wing actor Mark Ruffalo’s statements about Trump.

Finally, some attendees of the dinner have been disturbed by the lack of public response to the incident – with one reporter pontificating about how it shows how “desensitized” Americans are to political violence, even in the nation’s capital.

How can you express such a complaint after attending a dinner warmly hosting a man who’s brought so much violence to the world – whose administration turns airstrikes into memes – and whose allies cheer it on or laugh or look away?

Frankly, if you unhesitatingly attended the dinner just days after Israel – which Trump stands side-by-side with – killed our press colleague Amal Khalil in Lebanon, you are further desensitizing Americans to violence.

But what response does the Washington elite expect? After mass killing and mass killing – here and abroad – people have been made to feel powerless. No matter how many thousands of kids demand gun reform, no matter how many tens of thousands protest against the bombing of children, the response from DC politicians is effectively what Trump told Iowans after a school shooting in 2024: “We have to get over it.”

While political and media elites are dropping everything to discuss violence now that they got a whiff of it, the violence they’ve tolerated and ignored has brought us to this moment.

Just last week, there was a mass shooting in Republican House Speaker Mike Johnson’s hometown of Shreveport, Louisiana. Afterward, Gabby Giffords sought to meet with Johnson to discuss gun reform. He refused to meet with her, days before a gunman attempted to breach the swanky dinner he was attending.

Just hours after the dinner, five people were shot near Indiana University. That incident, naturally, did not drive nearly the level of news coverage of what we’ve seen since the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.

Ultimately, even amid the chaos at the gala event, some attendees seemed quite desensitized as well. As the Times reported, Goldman Sachs’s senior chairman Lloyd Blankfein asked a tablemate, “Are you going to finish that salad?” Another attendee, a top talent agent, went viral for continuing to eat his salad, too. One attendee was filmed grabbing bottles of wine on the way out.

The White House Correspondents’ Dinner ended early, but the after-parties continued. Why wouldn’t they?

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian state media on Sunday reported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks killed 3,468 people.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,509 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 35 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

New Iran proposal: Tehran has proposed a plan to open the Strait of Hormuz, end the US blockade, and end the war, while punting talks over its nuclear program to a later date, Axios reported. Such a plan is unlikely to be supported by Trump.

Iran-Russia talks: Iranian Foreign Affairs Minister Abbas Aragchi is in Russia for diplomatic talks on a range of issues, including the war. He is scheduled to meet with Vladimir Putin

Lebanon strikes: The Israeli military issued so-called evacuation orders for seven towns in southern Lebanon on Sunday and launched a barrage of missiles, killing more than a dozen people and continuing to flatten entire villages, despite the ceasefire. The Israeli military said one soldier was killed in Hezbollah attacks on its forces in southern Lebanon.

Solar panels targeted: Israeli forces destroyed solar panels that powered the water station and electricity to the Lebanese Christian town of Debl, and attacked homes, roads, and olive trees. It is the same town where an Israeli soldier broke a statue of Jesus just days earlier.

Trump attacks press: Trump lashed out at CBS’s Norah O’Donnell in a ‘60 Minutes’ interview Sunday after she asked for his reaction to an alleged manifesto from the White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting suspect, which read: “I’m no longer willing to permit a pedophile, rapist, and traitor to coat my hands with his crimes.” Trump called the interviewer “a disgrace,” and said, “I’m not a rapist.”

Trump v. IRS: The judge overseeing Trump’s ridiculous $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS for the leaking of his tax returns questioned whether the president can sue the US government that he himself oversees. The judge set a hearing for May 27.

What First Amendment? The New York Times reported that ICE could deny someone a green card for participating in campus protests or for a social media post that says “Stop Israeli Terror in Palestine.”

Michigan primary heats up: At the first debate on Friday, Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed attacked his opponent, state Senator Mallory McMorrow, over Drop Site’s reporting last year that she drafted an AIPAC position paper in what appeared to be an attempt to win over the Israel lobby.

General election starts in Maine? With just over one month left in Maine’s Democratic primary for Senate, Susan Collins’s super PAC launched $2 million worth of attack ads against Graham Platner, the veteran and oysterman who’s leading Governor Janet Mills in the polls.

Trusting Trump’s DOJ: Republican Senator Thom Tillis said he’s taking Trump’s Justice Department “at its word” that its politicized probe into Jerome Powell is closed, even though Trump said, “it’s not dropped.” Tillis will now vote to confirm Kevin Warsh as Fed chair, paving the way for his confirmation.

When was the first White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Palestinians in central Gaza’s Deir Al-Balah and the occupied West Bank voted in the first municipal elections since Israel’s genocide began, and loyalists of President Mahmoud Abbas won most races (Hamas did not nominate candidates in Deir Al-Balah and boycotted the election in the West Bank). Al Jazeera’s Hind Khoudary reported that voter turnout in Gaza was low, as people were “busy with surviving.”

🇲🇱 Mali minister dead: A Mali government spokesperson confirmed on Sunday that insurgents had killed Defense Minister Sadio Camara one day earlier at his house. West Africa’s al-Qaeda affiliate claimed responsibility for the attack.

🇸🇾 Assad-era officials on trial: Crowds gathered outside a Damascus courthouse on Sunday for the first public trial of Assad-era officials. Bashar Al-Assad’s cousin, Atef Najib, who appeared in court for “crimes against the Syrian people,” was the head of political security in the southern Deraa province when teenagers who drew anti-government graffiti were arrested and tortured, a case that catalyzed mass protests.

🇺🇦🇷🇺 Russia-Ukraine latest: Russian attacks on the central Ukrainian city of Dnipro killed five people and injured 46 others, authorities said Saturday. In Russia, local officials said a Ukrainian drone attack killed two people in the area of Belgorod, a border city.

🇨🇦 Altman apologizes: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman apologized to the people of Tumbler Ridge, a town in British Columbia, Canada, after admitting that the OpenAI account of a mass shooter who killed eight people had been flagged, but the company opted not to alert law enforcement. BC Premier David Ebby called the apology “necessary, and yet grossly insufficient.”


RFK Jr. rushed out of the White House Correspondents’ Dinner on Saturday amid an active shooter situation, with his security team around him, but left his wife scrambling on her own behind him. Not a good look, Bobby…

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said he’s “not ruling anything out” when CNN’s Dana Bash asked whether he will vacate the seditious conspiracy convictions secured against members of the Proud Boys and Oath Keepers stemming from the Jan. 6 Capitol riot. He also said Trump was within his rights and “his duty” in pardoning the Jan. 6 insurrectionists.

Meanwhile, on CBS’s ‘Face the Nation,’ Blanche said of gun reform: “I don’t think that’s something that we should be focused on right now in any way, shape, or form.”

Speaking about the recent Democratic redistricting measure that passed in Virginia, Rep. Ro Khanna said on NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’: “In a game that the Republicans started, they’re actually losing. They’re down a seat. And so the Democrats still would welcome an effort to ban gerrymandering.”

On Fox’s ‘Sunday Morning Futures,’ GOP Senator Ron Johnson said it’s a good time to “nuke the filibuster for good,” to pass a funding bill for the Homeland Security Department, while maintaining that it would be a “legitimate point of view” to keep the filibuster if Republicans had been in the minority.

Monday, April 27: Britain’s King Charles is set to arrive in the US on a state visit. Plus, in a landmark case, the Supreme Court is scheduled to weigh the legality of geotracking warrants to find criminals. Separately, a US district judge is set to hear Elon Musk’s lawsuit over OpenAI’s for-profit conversion.

Tuesday, April 28: A London Appeals Court is set to hear the British government’s case for upholding its designation of Palestine Action as a terrorist organization after the High Court ruled the proscription was unlawful. Plus, King Charles is set to address a joint meeting of Congress. Meanwhile, US Federal Reserve officials are scheduled to meet for two days to discuss their response to the ongoing oil crisis.

Wednesday, April 29: The Supreme Court is set to weigh Trump’s bid to terminate legal protections for Syrian and Haitian migrants.

Friday, May 1: Marches are scheduled around the world to mark International Workers’ Day, also known as May Day.

Zeteo’s Minnah Arshad, Melanie Riehl, and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: 🇺🇸 The Land of Endless Political Violence
item.description: On this day in 2011, President Barack Obama released a copy of his long-form birth certificate to silence racist conspiracy theorists, including Donald Trump. “I am really honored, and I am really proud that I was able to do something that nobody else could do,” Trump responded. Good morning,…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2011, President Barack Obama released a copy of his long-form birth certificate to silence racist conspiracy theorists, including Donald Trump. “I am really honored, and I am really proud that I was able to do something that nobody else could do,” Trump responded.

Good morning, friends. Prem and Andrew here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday morning wherever you are. Today, we need to talk about “incitement,” political violence, and who is actually desensitized to violence: the American people, or Washington, DC?

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we review the bizarre DC political and media response to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner gunman, Israel’s violence in Lebanon shatters any illusion of a “ceasefire,” and the Senate Democratic primary races in Maine and Michigan heat up.

“I’ve been coming to DC for almost 16 years, trying to make change. And I’m hoping this is the last time I have to come.” That’s what Judi Richardson, who lost her daughter to gun violence in 2010, said in front of the US Capitol last week. Richardson was among several family members of gun victims whom Prem followed around Congress as they begged our elected leaders to finally do something about America’s unique and horrifying epidemic.

They only had a handful of meetings, and the visit drew little attention. There was no momentum to be found on Capitol Hill for gun reform last week.

Days later, across the city, a gunman attempted to breach the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, with multiple shots being fired by law enforcement as he ran through the security perimeter inside the Washington Hilton. The incident – which took place at a glitzy media gala attended by President Donald Trump, his top officials, and titans of industries – set off a firestorm of controversy.

Trump and Republicans are using the shooting as fodder to support building the president’s pet project – a massive, corporate-funded White House ballroom. Even before Trump addressed the public, the talking points already seemed to be circulating.

“I don’t want to hear one more fucking criticism of Trump’s new ballroom at the White House,” conservative pundit Meghan McCain tweeted, setting off an avalanche of similar tweets from right-wing influencers and politicians – even though, to be clear, the White House Correspondents’ Dinner is a media event, hosted by an independent organization, and is unlikely to be hosted in the White House ballroom, were it to be completed.

We’re also seeing right-wing politicians and media figures laboring to blame the apparent assassination attempt on rhetoric from Democrats. And we’re seeing concerns from Beltway journalists about how Americans – who live in a nation with more guns than people, and the only country in the world where mass shootings are so commonplace – have been “desensitized” to “political violence.”

What planet have these people been living on?

Among the examples of supposedly worrying rhetoric from Democrats: Michigan Senator Elissa Slotkin called Trump an “existential threat to democracy” (which is correct), and Minnesota Governor Tim Walz described Trump as “fascist to his core” (fact check: also true). The Republican National Committee accused both of incitement, just weeks after Trump called Democrats “terrorists” (again) and threatened the genocide of 93 million people in Iran.

Montana Senator Tim Sheehy, for his part, accused the New York Times of “amplifying Islamic terrorists who demand violence against people they disagree with.” This is the same senator who, last month, helped break the arm of Brian McGinnis – a veteran who went to Congress to protest the US-Israeli war on Iran.

CNN’s Dana Bash (who herself has viciously compared pro-Palestine students to Nazis) on Sunday asked Democratic Rep. Jamie Raskin if he’s concerned about Democrats using rhetoric such as that Trump is “terrible for this country and so on.”

Of course, there’s no evidence that Democrats or the left are responsible for incidents like this. And Trump’s past alleged assailants have a mixed background. Thomas Matthew Crooks, who grazed Trump’s ear with a bullet in Butler, Pennsylvania, was a registered Republican.

Political violence is, in reality, a bipartisan issue - though studies show it is more of a right-wing than a left-wing problem. Minnesota Democratic lawmaker Melissa Hortman was murdered last year by a Trump supporter. In 2011, Democratic Rep. Gabby Giffords was shot in the head by a murderous far-right conspiracist. A left-wing activist shot GOP House Majority Leader Steve Scalise in 2017. Right-wing podcaster Charlie Kirk was assassinated at a college event in Utah last year.

Trump, for his part, is America’s inciter-in-chief. He regularly accuses Democratic politicians of treason and sedition. He has called for Democratic lawmakers to be executed.
